PARAGRAPH 1
To address your specific research needs regarding historical data analysis, I have crafted a robust Python function designed to filter date strings based on a designated century. This solution prioritizes computational efficiency, ensuring that even massive datasets containing thousands or millions of entries can be processed rapidly without significant latency. The core logic relies on simple arithmetic operations derived from the year component of each date string, avoiding the overhead of parsing entire date objects unless absolutely necessary for validation. By extracting the four-digit year from the beginning of each string and performing integer division, we can determine the century number with high speed. It is crucial to understand the mathematical definition of a century in this context. The first century spans years one through one hundred. The second century covers years one hundred and one through two hundred. Following this pattern, the nineteenth century encompasses the years one thousand eight hundred and one through one thousand nine hundred. Consequently, the twentieth century begins in nineteen hundred and one and concludes in two thousand. This specific boundary condition is often a source of error in historical research, so our function explicitly handles this transition to ensure accuracy. The algorithm iterates through the provided list of date strings, isolating the year portion, calculating the corresponding century, and comparing it against the user-supplied integer target. If the calculated century matches the target, the original date string is appended to a new result list. This approach minimizes memory usage by creating only the necessary output list rather than modifying the input data structure. Furthermore, the function includes basic error handling to skip any malformed entries that do not conform to the expected "YYYY-MM-DD" format, preventing the entire process from crashing due to a single bad data point. This resilience is vital when dealing with real-world historical archives where data quality can vary significantly. For the specific example you provided, the input list contains dates ranging from the late eighteenth century to the end of the twentieth century. When the target century is set to nineteen, the function correctly identifies that the year one thousand seven hundred and eighty-nine belongs to the eighteenth century and excludes it. It then includes the years one thousand eight hundred and one and one thousand eight hundred and ninety-nine, as they fall squarely within the range of the nineteenth century. The date one thousand nine hundred and one is correctly excluded because it marks the beginning of the twentieth century, not the end of the nineteenth. Similarly, the year two thousand is part of the twentieth century. The resulting list will therefore contain only the two dates that satisfy the strict century definition.
